name: Cloudflare D1 API
what_it_does: Provides a managed SQLite-compatible relational database that runs at the edge and is accessible from Cloudflare Workers via REST API or Workers bindings.
use_cases: Agent persists structured workflow state across invocations using SQL queries from a Worker
Agent queries a product catalog stored in D1 to answer user questions without round-tripping to a central DB
Agent executes batch SQL mutations to update records after completing a multi-step task
Agent creates and populates ephemeral D1 databases for per-user or per-session data isolation
Agent uses D1 as a lightweight task queue, inserting jobs and polling for completion status
not_for: High-throughput OLAP workloads or analytical queries over millions of rows
Applications requiring MySQL/PostgreSQL-specific features like stored procedures or advanced JSON operators
Teams not already using Cloudflare Workers as their compute layer
best_when: Your agent logic runs inside Cloudflare Workers and needs a co-located relational store with zero cold-start latency.
avoid_when: You need a database accessible from non-Cloudflare runtimes or require strong multi-region write consistency.

auth.notes: REST API uses Cloudflare API tokens with D1 permissions; within Workers, D1 is accessed via environment bindings with no additional auth required.

pricing.free_tier_limits: 5 million rows read/day, 100K rows written/day, 5 GB storage total
pricing.paid_tiers: Workers Paid plan ($5/month) includes 25 billion rows read/month, 50 million rows written/month, 25 GB storage; additional reads $0.001/million, writes $1.00/million
pricing.notes: Free tier is generous for development; production workloads typically need paid plan. Pricing is per-account across all D1 databases.

performance.rate_limits: No published per-database rate limit; throughput constrained by Worker CPU limits (10ms free, 30s paid) and SQLite single-writer model

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: SQL errors surface with SQLite error codes; REST API errors return structured JSON with Cloudflare error codes, but D1-specific error messages can be cryptic for constraint violations.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: SQL INSERT OR REPLACE and upsert patterns are supported; no built-in idempotency keys on the REST layer — agents must implement idempotency via SQL logic.
known_agent_gotchas: D1 uses SQLite's single-writer model, so concurrent writes from multiple Worker instances can cause 'SQLITE_BUSY' errors requiring retry logic
The REST API (via /client/v4/accounts/{id}/d1/database/{id}/query) is separate from the Workers binding — agents using REST need account ID and database ID in every request
SQLite type affinity can cause unexpected coercion (e.g., storing '123' as integer) — use explicit CAST in queries
D1 does not support WAL mode toggling or ATTACH DATABASE — some SQLite tooling assumptions will break
Row size and result set limits exist (1 MB per row, 10 MB per query result) — bulk data operations must be chunked

security_score_components.security_notes: API tokens support fine-grained permission scopes. Worker bindings avoid credential exposure in code. No row-level security — access control must be implemented in application logic.
